top_categories.workout_context: Race-pace efforts that mimic Tour de l'Île de Montréal's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before May.
31 miles on flat paved city streets and bike paths burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
When someone attacks on flat paved city streets and bike paths, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Tour de l'Île de Montréal's flat paved city streets and bike paths forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 31 miles.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what Tour de l'Île de Montréal's flat paved city streets and bike paths actually does to you.
2-minute all-out efforts on flat paved city streets and bike paths. Closing gaps, responding to attacks, sprinting for position. Train the burn.
Tour de l'Île de Montréal's flat paved city streets and bike paths rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Tour de l'Île de Montréal. Train it until it feels boring.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Tour de l'Île de Montréal, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Base fitness for Tour de l'Île de Montréal. Without this, the hard workouts break you down instead of building you up.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
5-second power closes gaps and wins field sprints. At Tour de l'Île de Montréal, neuromuscular snap is a tactical tool, not just a finish-line move.

race_overlay.nutrition: Standard hydration and fueling. One bottle per hour minimum. Gel or bar every 45 minutes at race intensity.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Race Simulation, Durability, and VO2max to prepare you for 31 miles of flat paved city streets and bike paths in Montreal, Quebec, Canada.
